ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese pork belly papaya salad. FOODON_00002809 (pork dish) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Burmese pork belly papaya salad. A0CKP (Pork meat products) is used as the closest defensible parent. The papaya salad component is not captured in this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Burmese pork belly papaya salad as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of shredded green papaya salad topped with sliced or chopped grilled pork belly, as typically served at a Burmese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component ingredients: USDA FoodData Central — Pork belly, raw (FDC ID 168375) and Green papaya, raw (analogous to raw papaya, FDC ID 169926); FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian foods (papaya salad component)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for Burmese pork belly papaya salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central data for pork belly (raw, FDC ID 168375) and raw papaya (FDC ID 169926), with fish sauce and lime juice as minor contributors. Approximate dish composition assumed: 50% pork belly (cooked weight), 40% green papaya, 10% dressing/condiments. Cooking losses applied for pork belly (approximately 30% fat reduction from grilling). Values represent a best-estimate per 100 g of the combined dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of green papaya and mixed salad dishes — analogous estimate based on raw green papaya GI (approximately 40–50, low GI) and pork belly (GI effectively 0 as a protein-fat food); composite dish GI estimated at 45
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese pork belly papaya salad. Green papaya (raw) has a GI of approximately 40–50 per the University of Sydney GI Database (low GI category). Pork belly contributes negligible glycemic load as a protein-fat food. The composite dish GI is estimated at 45 (low), weighted by carbohydrate contribution from the papaya and dressing components. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 16.5) / 100 ≈ 7.4 (serving size 300 g, carbohydrate_g per 100 g = 5.5 g, total carbohydrate per serving = 16.5 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and dried shrimp
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 300 g serving provides approximately 1260 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 45 (low) and approximately 16.5 g of carbohydrate per serving, this dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions, though the high sodium content from fish sauce warrants attention.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork belly and high sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The pork belly component contributes significant saturated fat (approximately 13.5 g per serving) and the fish sauce contributes high sodium, both of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals should limit portion frequency and consider leaner protein substitutions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Belly Papaya Salad (ဝက်ကင် papaya ထောင်း) is a Burmese dish combining grilled or roasted pork belly with shredded green papaya salad, dressed with fish sauce, lime juice, chilli, garlic, tomato, and dried shrimp. The dish is a fusion of the rich, fatty character of grilled pork belly with the crisp, tangy freshness of green papaya, creating a contrast of textures and flavours typical of Burmese cuisine. The pork belly is typically grilled until the skin is slightly charred and the fat is rendered, then sliced or chopped and served atop or alongside the papaya salad. The salad component is similar in style to Southeast Asian green papaya salads such as Thai som tam, but with distinctly Burmese seasoning. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (approximately 185 kcal per 100 g), high in protein and fat from the pork belly, and relatively low in carbohydrates from the green papaya. The sodium content is notably high due to fish sauce and dried shrimp, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (approximately 45), making it broadly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in standard portions. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free. It is served at Burmese restaurants in Singapore, including Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Burmese pork belly papaya salad as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for pork belly and raw papaya, with reasonable assumptions about dish composition and cooking losses. GI is an analogous estimate based on green papaya GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients (USDA FoodData Central: pork belly FDC ID 168375, raw papaya FDC ID 169926) using an assumed dish composition of approximately 50% cooked pork belly, 40% green papaya, and 10% dressing/condiments. Cooking losses for pork belly grilling are applied (approximately 30% fat reduction). These are best-estimate values and should be validated against laboratory analysis if this dish is to be used in a clinical or regulatory context. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the component sources used. 3. The input cuisine_tags included 'Noodle' and 'Lor Mee', which are inconsistent with this dish and have been disregarded as likely data entry errors in the field survey seed record. 4. The food_group in the input was listed as 'Vegetables', which has been corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' as pork belly is the primary protein component. 5. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Sodium content is high due to fish sauce and dried shrimp; this is a significant concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium uncertainty. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources.
